content:
A white Gaussian noise $w(t)$ with zero mean and power spectral density $\frac{N_0}{2}$,

when applied to a first-order RC low pass filter produces an output $n(t)$. At a particular time $t = t_k$, the variance of the random variable $n(t_k)$ is ________.
explanation:
Given : White Gaussian noise $W(t)$ with zero mean and power spectral density $\frac{N_0}{2}$.

(i) $\quad E[w(t)]=0$

(ii) $\quad S_W(f)=\frac{N_0}{2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Transfer function of RC Low pass filter is }\\ &H(f)=\frac{1}{1+j 2 \pi f R C} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Mean of output noise $n(t)$ is

$$ E[n(t)]=E[w(t)] H(0) $$

                                           [Given $E[w(t)]=0]$

$$ E[n(t)]=0 $$

Hence, mean of output noise is also zero.

Variance of output noise $n(t)$ is,

$$ \operatorname{Var}[n(t)]=E\left[n^2(t)\right]-[E\{n(t)\}]^2 $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \operatorname{Var}[n(t)]=E\left[n^2(t)\right]-0 \\ & \operatorname{Var}[n(t)]=E\left[n^2(t)\right] \end{aligned} $$

At $t=t_k$,

$$ \operatorname{Var}\left[n\left(t_k\right)\right]=E\left[n^2\left(t_k\right)\right] $$

Thus, Variance of output noise at $t=t_k$ is the mean square value of output noise at $t=t_k$

Since mean square value is equal to power of output noise.

$\operatorname{Var}\left[n\left(t_k\right)\right]=E\left[n^2\left(t_k\right)\right]=$ Power of output noise            ...(ii)

Calculation of power spectral density of output noise

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Power spectral density of output noise is }\\ &\begin{aligned} & S_n(f)=S_w(f)|H(f)|^2 \\ & S_n(f)=\frac{N_0}{2}|H(f)|^2 \\ & S_n(f)=\frac{N_0}{2}\left[\frac{1}{1+4 \pi^2 f^2 R^2 C^2}\right] \\ & S_n(f)=\frac{N_0}{2} \frac{1}{4 \pi^2 R^2 C^2}\left[\frac{1}{\frac{1}{4 \pi^2 R^2 C^2}+f^2}\right] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Power of output noise is, $P_n=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_n(f) d f$

Substituting the value of $S_n(f)$ from equation

(ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & P_n=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{N_0}{2} \frac{1}{4 \pi^2 R^2 C^2}\left[\frac{1}{\frac{1}{4 \pi^2 R^2 C^2}+f^2}\right] d f \\ & P_n=\frac{N_0}{2} \frac{1}{4 \pi^2 R^2 C^2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left[\frac{1}{\frac{1}{(2 \pi R C)^2+f^2}}\right] d f \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} P_n & =\frac{N_0}{2} \frac{1}{4 \pi^2 R^2 C^2} \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2 \pi R C}} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{f}{2 \pi R C}\right]_{-\infty}^{\infty} \\ P_n & =\frac{N_0}{2} \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}[\pi]=\frac{N_0}{4 R C} \\ P_n & =\frac{N_0}{4 R C} \end{aligned} $$

From equation (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & \operatorname{Var}\left[n\left(t_k\right)\right]=E\left[n^2\left(t_k\right)\right]=\text { Power of output noise } \\ & \operatorname{Var}\left[n\left(t_k\right)\right]=\frac{N_0}{4 R C} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct option is (A).

content: For a real signal, which of the following is/are valid power spectral density/densities?
explanation:
Based on the properties of power spectral density, $ S_X(-\omega) = S_X(\omega) $ for all $\omega$, and $ S_X(\omega) \geq 0 $ for all $\omega$.

Consider option (A):

$ S_X(\omega) = \frac{2}{9 + \omega^2} $

For any value of $\omega$,

$ S_X(\omega) \text{ is positive}, \text{ so } S_X(\omega) > 0 $

$ S_X(-\omega) = \frac{2}{9 + (-\omega)^2} = \frac{2}{9 + \omega^2} = S_X(\omega) $

Therefore, option (A) is a valid power spectral density.

Consider option (B) :

$$ S_X(\omega)=e^{-\omega^2} \cos ^2 \omega $$

At $\omega=\frac{\pi}{2}, S_X\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=e^{-\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2} \cos ^2 \frac{\pi}{2}=0$

For any other value of $\omega$, $S_X(\omega)$ is positive. So, $S_X(\omega) \geq 0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & S_X(-\omega)=e^{-(-\omega)^2} \cos ^2(\omega)=e^{-\omega^2} \cos ^2 \omega \\\\ & S_X(-\omega)=S_X(\omega) \end{aligned} $$

So, option (B) is a valid power spectral density.

Consider option (C) :

From the above plot, $S_X(\omega)$ is negative for some values of $\omega$ and odd function.

So, option $(\mathrm{C})$ is not a valid power spectral density.

Consider option (D) :

From the above plot, $S_X(\omega)$ is positive but not an even function, that is $S_X(\omega) \geq 0$ but $S_X(-\omega) \neq S_X(\omega)$.

So, option (D) is not a valid power spectral density.

Hence, the correct options are (A) and (B).

content:
The frequency response H(f) of a linear time-invariant system has magnitude as shown in the figure.

Statement I : The system is necessarily a pure delay system for inputs which are bandlimited to $$-$$$$\alpha$$ $$\le$$ f $$\le$$ $$\alpha$$.

Statement II : For any wide-sense stationary input process with power spectral density SX(f), the output power spectral density SY(f) obeys SY(f) = SX(f) for $$-$$$$\alpha$$ $$\le$$ f $$\le$$ $$\alpha$$.

Which one of the following combinations is true?

options.content: Statement I is correct, Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct, Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect, Statement II is correct
Statement I is incorrect, Statement II is incorrect

explanation:
$$Y(f) = X(f)\,.\,{e^{ - j2\pi f{T_d}}}$$

$$H(f) = {{Y(f)} \over {X(f)}} = {e^{ - j2\pi f{T_d}}}$$

$$|H(f)|\, = \,1$$

$$\angle H(f) = - 2\pi f{T_d}$$

Given that input is bandlimited to $$ - \alpha \le f \le \alpha $$.

Magnitude response of the system given

Based on the magnitude response given ; statement (I) is correct.

Note : Phase response of the system is not given in the question without knowing phase response of the system we can't comment about exact nature of the system.

$${(PSD)_{o/p}} = {(PSD)_{i/p}}\,.\,|H(f){|^2}$$

$${S_Y}(f) = {S_X}(f)$$ ; $$ - \alpha \le f \le \alpha $$

Statement (II) is correct.

Hence option (a) is correct.

content:
The autocorrelation function $R_X(\tau)$ of a wide-sense stationary random process $X(t)$ is shown in the figure.

$$ \text { The average power of } X(t) \text { is ___________} $$
explanation:
$$ \text { From question, we have } $$

We can say that the Average power of $x(t)$ is given as $=$ ?

$$ \begin{array}{rlrl} & & R_{x x}(\tau) & =E[x(t) \times(t+\tau)] \\ \text { or, } & & R_{x x}(0) & =E\left[x^2(t)\right] \\ \text { or, } & && =P_{\text {avg }}=2 \text { watts. } \end{array} $$

Hence, the correct answer is (2).

content:
The random variable

$$ Y=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W(t) \phi(t) d t, \quad \text { where } \phi(t)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} 1, & 5 \leq t \leq 7 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise } \end{array}\right. $$

and $W(t)$ is a real white Gaussian noise process with two-sided power spectral density $S_W(f)=3 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{Hz}$, for all $f$. The variance of $Y$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
explanation:
$$ \begin{gathered} \text { Given : } Y=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} W(t) \phi(t) d t \quad \text { where } \phi(t)= \begin{cases}1, & 5 \leq t \leq 7 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases} \\ S_W(f)=3 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{Hz} \end{gathered} $$

As $\phi(t)$ exists only for $5 \leq t \leq 7$, so the product of $W(t)$ and $\phi(t)$ will also exist for this duration only.

$$ \therefore \quad Y=\int_5^7 W(t) d t $$

Where, $W(t)=$ white Gaussian Noise with PSD $\frac{\eta}{2}=3 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{Hz}$.

So $Y$ represents a white Gaussian noise truncated in interval 5 to 7 .

As mean of WGN is zero, so the power will be equal to the variance of $Y$.

Variance of WGN truncated in interval $T_1$ to $T_1+T$,

Variance $\left[\int_{T_1}^{T_1+T} W(t) d t\right]=\frac{\eta}{2} \times T$

Variance $\left[\int_5^{5+2} W(t) d t\right]=$ Variance $[Y]=3 \times 2=6$

Hence, the variance of $Y$ is 6 .

Consider a real-valued random process

$$ f(t)=\sum\limits_{n=1}^N a_n p(t-n T), $$

where $T>0$ and $N$ is a positive integer. Here, $p(t)=1$ for $t \in[0,0.5 T]$ and 0 otherwise. The coefficients $a_n$ are pairwise independent, zero-mean unit-variance random variables. Read the following statements about the random process and choose the correct option.

(i) The mean of the process $f(t)$ is independent of time $t$.

(ii) The autocorrelation function $E[f(t) f(t+\tau)]$ is independent of time $t$ for all $\tau$. (Here, $E[\cdot]$ is the expectation operation.)
explanation:
$$ \text { Given Random process } f(t)=\sum_{n=1}^N a_n p(t-n T) $$

$\left\{a_n\right\}$ specifies set of independent random variables each having zero mean and unit variance.

Mean:

$$ E[f(t)]=\sum_{n=1}^N E\left[a_n\right] p(t-n T) $$

Given that $E\left[a_n\right]=0$

$$ E[f(t)]=0 $$

$E[f(t)]$ is independent of $t$.

ACF:

$$ \operatorname{ACF}[f(t)]=\sum_{n=1}^n \operatorname{ACF}\left[a_n\right] \cdot \operatorname{ACF}[p(t-n T)] $$

Let $p(t-n T)=g(t)$ where $g(t)$ is a deterministic signal.

$$ \operatorname{ACF}[p(t-n T)]=\operatorname{ACF}[g(t)]=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(t) \cdot g(t+\tau) d t $$

From observation $\operatorname{ACF}[g(t)]$ is function of $t$. i.e., $\operatorname{ACF}[f(t)]$ is function of $t$.

content:
A source transmits a symbol $s$, taken from $\\{-4, 0, 4\\}$ with equal probability, over an additive white Gaussian noise channel. The received noisy symbol $r$ is given by $r = s + w$, where the noise $w$ is zero mean with variance 4 and is independent of $s$.

Using $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int\limits_{x}^{\infty} e^{-\frac{t^{2}}{2}} dt$, the optimum symbol error probability is _______.
explanation:
Given : Source transmit symbol $s$

Symbol set $s\{-4,0,4\}$

$$ s_1=-4, s_2=0, s_3=4 $$

and $P\left(s_1\right)=P\left(s_2\right)=P\left(s_3\right)=\frac{1}{3}$

Received noisy symbol $r$

$$ r=s+w $$

where ' $w$ ' is zero mean with variance 4 Gaussian random variable

$$ \sigma^2=4, \sigma=2 $$

Conditional probability density function for the transmitted symbols

$$ \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\, \,\,\,\,\,V_{t h}=-2 \quad V_{t h}=2 $$

Since, all the symbols are equiprobable, decision boundaries will be intersection points.

$$ \begin{aligned} & P_e\left[s_1\right]=P\left[r / s_1>-2\right]=Q\left(\frac{-2-\mu_{r / s_1}}{\sigma}\right)[\because \sigma=2] \\ & P\left[r / s_1>-2\right]=Q\left[\frac{-2-(-4)}{2}\right] \\ & P\left[r / s_1>-2\right]=Q\left(\frac{-2+4}{2}\right) \\ & P\left[r / s_1>-2\right]=Q(1) \\ & P_e\left[s_2\right]=P\left[r / s_2<-2\right]+P\left[r / s_2>2\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} = & {\left[1-Q\left(\frac{-2-\mu_{r / s_2}}{2}\right)\right]+Q\left[\frac{2-\mu_{r / s_2}}{2}\right] } \\ = & {[1-Q(-1)]+Q(1)=2 Q(1) } \\ P_e\left[s_3\right]= & P\left[r / s_3<2\right]=1-Q\left[\frac{2-\mu_{r / s_3}}{2}\right] \\ = & 1-Q(-1)=Q(1) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Optimum symbol error probability, }\\ &\begin{array}{r} P_e=\frac{1}{3} P_e\left(s_1\right)+\frac{1}{3} P_e\left(s_2\right)+\frac{1}{3} P_e\left(s_3\right) \\ =\frac{1}{3} Q(1)+\frac{1}{3} 2 Q(1)+\frac{1}{3} Q(1) \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &P_e=\frac{4}{3} Q(1)\\ &\text { Hence, the correct option is (B). } \end{aligned} $$

content:
Let $X(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t+\theta)$ be a random process, where amplitude $A$ and phase $\theta$ are independent of each other, and are uniformly distributed in the intervals $[-2,2]$ and $[0, 2\pi]$, respectively. $X(t)$ is fed to an 8-bit uniform mid-rise type quantizer.

Given that the autocorrelation of $X(t)$ is $R_X(\tau) = \frac{2}{3} \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$, the signal to quantization noise ratio (in dB, rounded off to two decimal places) at the output of the quantizer is _________.
explanation:
First, we need to determine the necessary parameters for the signal and the quantizer to find the Signal to Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) for the given scenario.

The signal $X(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t+\theta)$, where $A$ is uniformly distributed in the range $[-2, 2]$ and $\theta$ is uniformly distributed in the range $[0, 2\pi]$. This implies the amplitude $A$ ranges from -2 to 2, covering a total range of 4 units.

The quantizer is an 8-bit uniform mid-rise type, meaning it can encode the signal into $2^8 = 256$ levels. The mid-rise type quantizer implies the step size $\Delta$ between quantization levels can be found with:

$$\Delta = \frac{\text{Range of } A}{2^8} = \frac{4}{256} = \frac{1}{64}$$

Now, we need to calculate the power of the original signal and the quantization noise. Given the autocorrelation function:

$$R_X(\tau) = \frac{2}{3} \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$$

To find the signal power $P_X$, evaluate the autocorrelation function at $\tau = 0$:

$$P_X = R_X(0) = \frac{2}{3} \cos(0) = \frac{2}{3}$$

The quantization noise power $P_Q$ for a uniform quantizer is:

$$P_Q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{\left(\frac{1}{64}\right)^2}{12} = \frac{1}{4096 \cdot 12} = \frac{1}{49152}$$

The Signal to Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR) can be calculated by taking the ratio of the signal power to the noise power:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_X}{P_Q} = \frac{\frac{2}{3}}{\frac{1}{49152}} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 49152 = 32768$$

Finally, convert this SQNR to decibels (dB):

$$\text{SQNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10} \left( 32768 \right)$$

Using a calculator to find the logarithm:

$$\text{SQNR (dB)} = 10 \log_{10} \left( 32768 \right) \approx 10 \times 4.515 \approx 45.15 \, \text{dB}$$

Thus, the signal to quantization noise ratio (in dB, rounded off to two decimal places) at the output of the quantizer is 45.15.

content:
A random variable X, distributed normally as N(0, 1), undergoes the transformation Y = h(X), given in the figure. The form of the probability density function of Y is

(In the options given below, a, b, c are non-zero constants and g(y) is piece-wise continuous function)
explanation:
Given that the random variable $X$, undergoes the transformation $Y=h(X)$.

From the given figure, it can be concluded that, $Y$ takes the discrete set of values $\{-1,0,1\}$. So, probability density function of $Y$ will consist of impulses at $y=-1, y=0$ and $y=1$.

$\therefore$ The probability density function of $Y$ can be represented as,

$$ f(y)=a \delta[y+1]+b \delta[y]+c \delta[y-1]+g[y] $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: Let X(t) be a white Gaussian noise with power spectral density $$\frac{1}{2}$$W/Hz. If X(t) is input to an LTI system with impulse response $$e^{-t}u(t)$$. The average power of the system output is ____________ W (rounded off to two decimal places).
explanation:
To determine the average power of the system output, we need to follow these steps:

1. Understand the input and the system: We are given that the input $ X(t) $ is a white Gaussian noise with a power spectral density (PSD) $$\frac{1}{2}$$ W/Hz. The LTI system has an impulse response $$h(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$$, where $ u(t) $ is the unit step function.

2. Find the system's frequency response: The frequency response $ H(f) $ of an LTI system is the Fourier transform of the impulse response $ h(t) $.

For the given impulse response $ h(t) = e^{-t}u(t) $, the Fourier transform is:

$$ H(f) = \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t) e^{-j2\pi ft} \, dt = \int\limits_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t} e^{-j2\pi ft} \, dt $$

Solving the integral:

$$ H(f) = \int\limits_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t(1 + j2\pi f)} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-t(1 + j2\pi f)}}{-(1 + j2\pi f)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 + j2\pi f} $$

3. Determine the output power spectral density: The PSD of the output $ Y(t) $ of the system can be found using the input PSD and the magnitude square of the frequency response $ H(f) $. The output PSD $ S_Y(f) $ is given by:

$$ S_Y(f) = S_X(f) |H(f)|^2 $$

Given $ S_X(f) = \frac{1}{2} $ W/Hz, and from the previous step:

$$ |H(f)|^2 = \left| \frac{1}{1 + j2\pi f} \right|^2 = \frac{1}{(1 + (2\pi f)^2)} $$

Thus, the output PSD is:

$$ S_Y(f) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f)^2} $$

4. Calculate the average power of the system output: The average power of the system output can be found by integrating the output PSD over all frequencies:

$$ P_Y = \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_Y(f) \, df = \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f)^2} \, df $$

To solve this integral, we can use the substitution $ u = 2\pi f $, $ du = 2\pi df $, so $ \frac{1}{2\pi} du = df $. Thus, the integral becomes:

$$ P_Y = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f)^2} \, df = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1 + u^2} \cdot \frac{du}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{4\pi} \cdot \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1 + u^2} \, du $$

We know that:

$$ \int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{1 + u^2} \, du = \pi $$

Therefore, the average power is:

$$ P_Y = \frac{1}{4\pi} \cdot \pi = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ W} $$

So, the average power of the system output is 0.25 W (rounded off to two decimal places).

content:
Consider a real valued source whose samples are independent and identically distributed random variables with the probability density function, f(x), as shown in the figure.

Consider a 1 bit quantizer that maps positive samples to value $$\alpha$$ and others to value $$\beta$$. If $$\alpha$$* and $$\beta$$* are the respective choices for $$\alpha$$ and $$\beta$$ that minimize the mean square quantization error, then ($$\alpha$$* $$-$$ $$\beta$$*) = ___________ (rounded off to two decimal places).
explanation:
$${1 \over 2} \times K \times 2 + 1 \times K = 1 \Rightarrow K = {1 \over 2} = 0.5$$

$$ - 2 \le x \le 0 \to {f_X}(x) = mx + C$$

$${f_X}(x) = 0.25x + C$$

when $$x = - 2 \to {f_X}(x) = 0$$

$$0 = 0.25 \times - 2 + C \Rightarrow C = 0.5$$

$${f_X}(x) = {1 \over 4}x + {1 \over 2} = - 2 \le x \le 0$$

Quantizer output,

fX(x) = 0.5 ; 0 $$\le$$ x $$\le$$ 1

xq = $$\alpha$$ ; for 0 $$\le$$ x $$\le$$ 1

xq = $$\beta$$ ; for $$-$$2 $$\le$$ x $$\le$$ 0

Mean square quantization error,

$$MSQ[{Q_e}] = E[Q_e^2]$$

Qe = (Sampled value) $$-$$ (quantized value)

= X $$-$$ xq

$$MSQ[{Q_e}] = E[{(X - {x_q})^2}]$$

Quantization noise power, $${N_0} = MSQ[{Q_e}] = \int {{{(X - {x_q})}^2}{f_x}(x)dx} $$

For, $$ - 2 \le x \le 0 \to $$

$${N_Q} = \int\limits_{ - 2}^0 {{{(x - \beta )}^2} \times \left( {{1 \over 4}x + {1 \over 2}} \right)dx} $$

$$ = \int\limits_{ - 2}^0 {({x^2} + {\beta ^2} - 2x\beta )\left( {{x \over 4} + {1 \over 2}} \right)dx} $$

$${N_Q} = {{{\beta ^2}} \over 2} + {2 \over 3}\beta - {1 \over 3}$$

To find $$\beta$$ value for which NQ will be minimum $$\to$$

$${{{d_{NQ}}} \over {{d_\beta }}} = 0 \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} \times 2\beta + {2 \over 3} = 0$$

$$\beta = {{ - 2} \over 3}$$

For, $$0 \le x \le 1 \to $$

$${N_Q} = \int\limits_0^1 {{{(x - \alpha )}^2} \times {1 \over 2}dx} $$

$${N_Q} = {1 \over 6}\left[ {{{(1 - \alpha )}^3} + {\alpha ^3}} \right]$$

To find '$$\alpha$$' value for which NQ is minimum

$${{{d_{NQ}}} \over {d\alpha }} = 0 \Rightarrow {1 \over 6}\left[ {3{{(1 - \alpha )}^2}( - 1) + 3{\alpha ^2}} \right] = 0$$

$$\alpha = {1 \over 2}$$

Chosen $$\alpha$$* and $$\beta$$* for which mean square quantization error (NQ) is minimum will be $${1 \over 2}$$ and $${{ - 2} \over 3}$$ respectively.

$$\alpha * - \beta * = {1 \over 2} + {2 \over 3} = {7 \over 6} = 1.167$$

content:
Let $$X(t)$$ be a wide sense stationary random process with the power spectral density $${S_x}\left( f \right)$$ as shown in figure (a), where $$f$$ is in Hertz $$(Hz)$$. The random process $$X(t)$$ is input to an ideal low pass filter with the frequency response $$$H\left( f \right) = \left\{ {\matrix{ {1,} & {\left| f \right| \le {1 \over 2}Hz} \cr {0,} & {\left| f \right| > {1 \over 2}Hz} \cr } } \right.$$$

As shown in Figure (b). The output of the low pass filter is $$y(t)$$.

Let $$E$$ be the expectation operator and consider the following statements :
$$\left( {\rm I} \right)$$ $$E\left( {X\left( t \right)} \right) = E\left( {Y\left( t \right)} \right)$$
$$\left( {{\rm I}{\rm I}} \right)$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,E\left( {{X^2}\left( t \right)} \right) = E\left( {{Y^2}\left( t \right)} \right)$$
$$\left( {{\rm I}{\rm I}{\rm I}} \right)\,$$ $$\,\,\,\,\,\,E\left( {{Y^2}\left( t \right)} \right) = 2$$

Select the correct option:

options.content: only $${\rm I}$$$${\rm I}$$ and $${\rm I}$$$${\rm I}$$$${\rm I}$$ are true
only $${\rm I}$$ and $${\rm I}$$$${\rm I}$$ are true
only $${\rm I}$$  and $${\rm I}$$$${\rm I}$$$${\rm I}$$ are true

content: Consider random process $$X(t) = 3V(t) - 8$$, where $$V$$ $$(t)$$ is a zero mean stationary random process with autocorrelation $${R_v}\left( \tau  \right) = 4{e^{ - 5\left| \tau  \right|}}$$. The power of $$X(t)$$ is _______.
